quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 10 Quiz 8
quiz.subtitle: Using Area to Calculate the Likelihood of Hitting a Target Region
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: Geometric probability using area models is calculated as:
options: $\frac{\text{Area of target region}}{\text{Area of total region}}$
$\frac{\text{Area of total region}}{\text{Area of target region}}$
questions.explanation: Geometric probability is the ratio of the area of the desired region to the area of the entire region, assuming a random point is equally likely to land anywhere. $P = \frac{\text{target area}}{\text{total area}}$. This is always a number between $0$ and $1$.

questions.question: A dartboard is a square of side $20$ inches. The bullseye is a circle of radius $3$ inches at the center. If a dart hits the board at random, what is the probability it hits the bullseye? Leave your answer in terms of $\pi$.
questions.explanation: Total area $= 20^2 = 400$ in$^2$. Bullseye area $= \pi(3^2) = 9\pi$ in$^2$. $P = \frac{9\pi}{400} \approx 0.071$, or about $7.1\%$. The probability is fairly low because the bullseye is small compared to the entire board.

questions.question: A large rectangle measures $12$ cm by $8$ cm. A smaller rectangle measuring $6$ cm by $4$ cm is drawn inside it. If a point is chosen at random in the large rectangle, what is the probability it lands in the small rectangle?
questions.explanation: Large area $= 12 \times 8 = 96$ cm$^2$. Small area $= 6 \times 4 = 24$ cm$^2$. $P = \frac{24}{96} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 = 25\%$. The small rectangle occupies exactly one-quarter of the large rectangle.

questions.question: A square has side length $10$ inches. A right triangle occupies exactly half the square, with its legs along two sides of the square. What is the probability a random point in the square lands in the triangle?
questions.explanation: Triangle area $= \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10 = 50$ in$^2$. Square area $= 100$ in$^2$. $P = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5 = 50\%$. The triangle covers exactly half the square, so it's an even chance.

questions.question: A circle of radius $8$ cm has a shaded sector with central angle $90^\circ$. If a point is chosen at random inside the circle, what is the probability it lands in the shaded sector?
questions.explanation: The sector area $= \frac{90}{360} \times \pi(8^2) = \frac{1}{4} \times 64\pi = 16\pi$ cm$^2$. Full circle area $= 64\pi$ cm$^2$. $P = \frac{16\pi}{64\pi} = \frac{1}{4} = 25\%$. For sectors of the same circle, the probability equals the angle fraction.

questions.question: A circle of radius $5$ m has a shaded sector with central angle $72^\circ$. What is the probability a random point in the circle lands in the sector?
questions.explanation: $P = \frac{72}{360} = \frac{1}{5} = 20\%$. The $\pi r^2$ terms cancel because the sector and the full circle have the same radius. The probability depends only on the central angle as a fraction of $360^\circ$.

questions.question: A circular dartboard has radius $9$ inches. The outer ring (the region between radii $6$ and $9$ inches) scores $10$ points. What is the probability of hitting this outer ring? Express as a simplified fraction.
questions.explanation: Outer ring area $= \pi(9^2) - \pi(6^2) = 81\pi - 36\pi = 45\pi$ in$^2$. Total area $= 81\pi$ in$^2$. $P = \frac{45\pi}{81\pi} = \frac{45}{81} = \frac{5}{9} \approx 0.556$ or about $55.6\%$. More than half the dartboard is the outer ring.

questions.question: A circle of radius $4$ cm is inscribed in a square of side $8$ cm (the circle touches all four sides). What is the probability a random point in the square lands inside the circle? Leave in terms of $\pi$.
questions.explanation: Circle area $= 16\pi$ cm$^2$. Square area $= 64$ cm$^2$. $P = \frac{16\pi}{64} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785$ or $78.5\%$. This is a classic geometric probability problem — the ratio is always $\frac{\pi}{4}$ for a circle inscribed in a square.

questions.question: A rectangular field measures $100$ m by $60$ m. A circular landing zone of radius $15$ m is painted in the center. A skydiver lands at a random point on the field. What is the probability they land in the zone, rounded to the nearest percent?
questions.explanation: Field area $= 100 \times 60 = 6{,}000$ m$^2$. Zone area $= \pi(15^2) \approx 706.9$ m$^2$. $P = \frac{706.9}{6{,}000} \approx 0.1178 \approx 12\%$. The landing zone is relatively small compared to the entire field.

questions.question: A square garden has side length $20$ ft. A sprinkler at the center waters a circular region of radius $10$ ft. What fraction of the garden gets watered? Leave in terms of $\pi$.
questions.explanation: Garden area $= 20^2 = 400$ ft$^2$. Watered area $= \pi(10^2) = 100\pi$ ft$^2$. $P = \frac{100\pi}{400} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785$ or $78.5\%$. The sprinkler covers about $78.5\%$ of the garden — the corners remain dry.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered geometric probability using area models. You can calculate probabilities for circles, squares, sectors, rings, and real-world targets with both exact and approximate answers.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) $P = \frac{\text{target area}}{\text{total area}}$, (2) for sectors of the same circle, the $\pi r^2$ cancels and $P = \frac{\theta}{360^\circ}$, (3) for donut-shaped regions, subtract the inner area from the outer area, (4) probabilities are always between $0$ and $1$.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) identifying the target region and total region correctly, (2) using the right area formula for each shape, (3) simplifying fractions completely, (4) converting between exact forms ($\frac{\pi}{4}$) and approximate percents ($78.5\%$) when asked.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Geometric probability $= \frac{\text{target area}}{\text{total area}}$. For circles: $A = \pi r^2$. For squares/rectangles: $A = lw$. For sectors: $A = \frac{\theta}{360^\circ} \times \pi r^2$. For rings: subtract inner from outer. The answer can be left as a fraction, in terms of $\pi$, or as a decimal/percent.
